unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit you explore the tiny world of bugs, insects and other small garden animals. You learn the names of the butterfly, cricket, ant, bee, spider and worm, and you find out what makes an animal an insect: six legs, three body parts and two antennae. You learn position words such as on, under, between and above so that you can say exactly where a creature is, and you use the simple present tense to give true facts. By the end you can describe a creature of your own, just like Amal in this unit's story, “Amal and the Little Garden Friends”.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher lecture, then learn a few vocabulary words each day and look for each creature in a real garden or a picture book.
Work through the six grammar practices: position words, the simple present tense for facts, and asking questions with What, Where and How.
Read “What Is an Insect?”, the bug poem, the listening text “Grandpa's Cricket” and the story “Amal and the Little Garden Friends”, then answer the seventeen comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.
unit.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 2 Unit 6 source pack under docs/lesson-authoring-super-prompt.md
unit.reviewStatus: Pilot rebuild - pending curriculum reviewer sign-off

visual.alt: Cartoon children crouching in a garden, watching a butterfly, a bee, an ant, a spider and a worm.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: An insect with large, colourful wings that flies from flower to flower.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The butterfly landed on the red flower to drink nectar.
practiceSentences: A blue butterfly rested on the warm garden wall.
How many colours can you see on that butterfly?
“Look at the butterfly on the lemon tree,” said Adam.
I painted a butterfly with orange and black wings.
Every butterfly begins its life as a small caterpillar.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - u - t - t - e - r - f - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult and ask the tutor to describe a butterfly's wings, then draw what you hear.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small jumping insect that makes a chirping sound with its wings.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We heard a cricket chirping in the garden at night.
practiceSentences: The cricket jumped from the long grass onto my shoe.
Where does a cricket hide in the daytime?
“That cricket is louder than the radio,” laughed Mina.
I keep still so the cricket does not jump away.
One cricket can leap much further than its own body.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - r - i - c - k - e - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper beside you, ask the tutor to explain how a cricket makes its chirping sound.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small insect that lives in large groups and works with others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The ants carried food back to their anthill.
practiceSentences: One tiny ant can carry a seed bigger than itself.
Why does every ant follow the same narrow path?
“Do not step there, an ant is crossing,” said Amal.
I watched an ant climb over my shoe.
A hungry ant follows the smell of sweet fruit.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to sit with you, then ask the tutor what different jobs ants do inside their nest.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A flying insect that collects nectar from flowers and makes honey.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bee buzzed around the flowers collecting nectar.
practiceSentences: A striped bee visited every yellow flower in the garden.
How does a bee carry nectar back to its hive?
“Stay calm and the bee will fly away,” said Adam.
I stood very still while the bee passed my hand.
Without the bee, many plants would grow no fruit.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask the tutor why bees are important to a garden, then say one reason back.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small animal with eight legs that spins webs to catch insects.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The spider built a web between two branches.
practiceSentences: A garden spider waited quietly in the middle of its web.
How many legs does a spider have altogether?
“Count them, Mina, a spider has eight legs,” said Amal.
I moved the spider outside with a cup and a card.
A spider is not an insect, because it has eight legs.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - p - i - d - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to tell you one surprising fact about spiders while your helper listens with you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A long, soft animal with no legs that lives in the soil.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After the rain, we saw worms on the wet path.
practiceSentences: A fat worm wriggled slowly under the fallen leaf.
Does a worm have any legs at all?
“Please do not step on the worm,” called Amal.
I found a worm while I was digging the garden.
Every worm helps the soil so plants grow strong.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor how worms help plants grow, with an adult beside you, then tell your family what you learned.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small animal with six legs, three body parts and often wings.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A butterfly is an insect with beautiful, colourful wings.
practiceSentences: Every insect has six legs and two antennae.
Is a spider an insect or something different?
“Count the legs to check if it is an insect,” said Adam.
I drew an insect and labelled its three body parts.
An insect can be as small as a grain of rice.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - s - e - c - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to sit with you, then ask the tutor to name three insects you might find near your home.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The body parts that animals use for walking, standing or jumping.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Insects have six legs, but spiders have eight legs.
practiceSentences: The ant moved its six legs very quickly.
How many legs does a cricket use to jump?
“A worm has no legs at all,” explained Adam.
I counted the legs on the beetle in my book.
Long legs help a spider walk across its silky web.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper watching, ask the tutor to name a creature and then say how many legs it has.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The body parts that some insects and birds use for flying.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Butterflies have colourful wings, but ants do not always have wings.
practiceSentences: The bee beat its small wings faster than we could see.
Do all insects have wings on their backs?
“Its wings are folded shut,” whispered Mina.
I painted bright spots on my paper butterfly wings.
A cricket rubs its wings together to make a sound.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor which creatures in this unit have wings and which do not, and check your list with an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The two thin parts on an insect's head that help it feel and smell.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The butterfly's antennae help it find sweet flowers.
practiceSentences: The ant taps the ground with its antennae.
What do antennae help an insect find?
“Its antennae are longer than its body,” said Nora.
I drew two antennae on top of my insect's head.
Antennae work a little like a nose and fingers together.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - n - t - e - n - n - a - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult and ask the tutor how antennae help an insect find food in the dark.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The home that ants build, often with many rooms under the ground.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The busy ants ran in and out of the anthill.
practiceSentences: A small anthill rose beside the garden path.
How many ants can live inside one anthill?
“The whole family sleeps in that anthill,” said Adam.
I watched the anthill after the rain had stopped.
Rooms and tunnels fill an anthill under the soil.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - n - t - h - i - l - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to describe the rooms inside an anthill, then draw the picture in your head with an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The net of thin silk that a spider spins to catch insects.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A spider makes a strong web from silk inside its body.
practiceSentences: The web shone with tiny drops of morning rain.
How long does a spider take to spin a web?
“Do not break the web,” said Amal kindly.
I found a round web between the two branches.
A torn web is mended by the spider each night.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask the tutor to explain how a web can hold an insect without breaking.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Touching the top or the surface of something else.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The butterfly is on the flower.
practiceSentences: The cricket sat on a warm grey rock.
Is the ladybird on the leaf or under it?
“Your book is on the table,” said Mina.
I put my hand flat on the soft soil.
Two bees landed on the yellow sunflower together.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to hide a toy bug, then ask the tutor to check the sentence you say about where it sits.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: In a lower place than something else, and often covered by it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The worm is under the rock.
practiceSentences: A snail hides under the wide green leaf.
What is living under that old flowerpot?
“The ants are under the ground,” explained Adam.
I keep my boots under the garden bench.
Worms crawl under the soil and make it healthy.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper beside you, ask the tutor to name three places a worm could hide under.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Inside something else, with walls or sides all around it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The ant is in the anthill.
practiceSentences: The spider waits in the middle of its web.
Who is hiding in the tall grass?
“The cricket is in my pocket,” laughed Nora.
I keep my bug book in my school bag.
Bees sleep in the hive when night comes.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to explain the difference between on the box and in the box, with an adult listening too.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: In the middle of two other things, with one on each side.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The cricket is between two leaves.
practiceSentences: The web hangs between two branches of the lemon tree.
Can you see the ant between those two stones?
“Sit between Mina and me,” said Amal.
I planted a small flower between the two bushes.
A narrow path runs between the garden and the gate.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - t - w - e - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Stand between two chairs with an adult, then ask the tutor whether your sentence about your place is correct.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: In a higher place than something else, without touching it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bee is above the flower.
practiceSentences: A butterfly floated above the tall sunflowers.
What is flying above our heads?
“Hold the net above the bucket,” said Adam.
I watched the clouds move above the garden.
Bees hum above the flowers all morning long.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to explain why we say above and not on when nothing is touching, and repeat it to an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Just before something else, and closer to the person looking.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The spider is in front of its web.
practiceSentences: A big beetle stopped in front of my shoe.
Who is standing in front of the garden gate?
“Wait in front of the door,” called Mina.
I knelt in front of the anthill to watch.
The bench sits in front of the lemon tree.
dictionaryLinks.sentenceStarter: The spider is in front of
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - f - r - o - n - t - o - f
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Line up three toys with an adult, then ask the tutor to say which toy is in front of the others.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move through the air, usually by using wings.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Bees and butterflies can fly from flower to flower.
practiceSentences: Ladybirds open their spotted backs before they fly.
How high can a bee fly in one day?
“Watch the butterfly fly over the wall,” said Nora.
I like to watch the bees fly around our garden.
Ants cannot fly, but many other insects can.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor which creatures in this unit can fly, then check the answer with an adult and your word list.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To push off the ground so your body moves up.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A cricket can jump very high with its strong legs.
practiceSentences: Grasshoppers jump away when we walk through the grass.
Why does a cricket jump instead of walking?
“Do not jump on the flowerbed,” said Amal.
I can jump over the puddle by the gate.
Some tiny spiders jump to catch their food.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Count your own jumps with an adult, then ask the tutor to compare your jump with a cricket's jump.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move slowly along the ground or across a surface.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The worm can crawl through the soft, wet soil.
practiceSentences: Beetles crawl under the leaves to stay cool.
How fast can a caterpillar crawl?
“A ladybird is going to crawl onto your finger,” said Adam.
I watched an ant crawl along the edge of my book.
Worms crawl through the soil and help plants grow.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to name three creatures that crawl rather than fly, and say each one aloud to your helper.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make thread or a web by twisting fine silk.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Spiders spin webs to catch flying insects.
practiceSentences: A garden spider can spin a new web in one night.
How does a spider spin such fine silk?
“Watch her spin the last thread,” whispered Mina.
I watched a spider spin its web on the fence.
Some caterpillars spin a small case before they change.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask the tutor to describe the order in which a spider spins its web.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make a short, high sound, like a cricket at night.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Crickets chirp loudly in the garden at night.
practiceSentences: The crickets chirp when the sun goes down.
Why do crickets chirp so loudly after dark?
“Listen, I can hear one cricket chirp,” said Nora.
I fall asleep to the chirp of the crickets.
Birds chirp in the morning, and crickets chirp at night.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to explain what a cricket is saying when it chirps, and talk about the answer with an adult.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To gather things together and bring them to one place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Bees collect nectar from flowers to make honey.
practiceSentences: Ants collect seeds and carry them to the anthill.
How much nectar can one bee collect in a day?
“Let us collect leaves for the bug hotel,” said Leo.
I collect pictures of insects in my notebook.
Worker bees collect pollen on their furry legs.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - l - l - e - c - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Collect five leaves outside with an adult, then ask the tutor to help you describe what you collected.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name common bugs and garden animals: butterfly, cricket, ant, bee, spider and worm.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: The six creature names said correctly in the Speaking 1 recording, and all six matched to their homes in Activity 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what makes an animal an insect: six legs, three body parts and two antennae.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct answers in Activity 1, and a fact card in Writing 4 with all five insect parts labelled.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain why a spider and a worm are not insects.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct True or False answers with corrections in Activity 2, and two because sentences in Writing 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use position words (on, under, in, between, above, in front of) to say where a bug is.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Six sentences in Writing 2 that use each position word once, and six position-word clues in Activity 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the simple present tense to give true facts, such as “Bees make honey.”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct verb forms in Grammar 2, and six fact sentences with the right verb endings in Grammar 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask and answer questions with What, Where and How.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three correct question words in Grammar 3, and six planned questions asked in the Speaking 6 question circle.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write simple descriptions of bugs and the places where they live.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A three or four sentence bug description in Writing 1, and a six-sentence garden story in Writing 6.

readings.title: What Is an Insect?
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Insects are small animals with special features. All insects have six legs. They have three main body parts: the head, the thorax, and the abdomen. Most insects have two antennae on their heads. Antennae help insects feel and smell things around them. Many insects have wings, but not all of them. Butterflies, bees, and crickets are all insects. Spiders are not insects, because they have eight legs. Worms are not insects, because they have no legs at all. There are more kinds of insects in the world than any other type of animal!

readings.title: A Bug Poem
readings.passageScript:
Read this short poem, then try to say it from memory:
One, two, three!
There's a bug on me.
Where did it go?
I don't know.

readings.title: Grandpa’s Cricket
readings.passageScript:
Listen to Nora talk with her grandpa. Ask an adult to read it aloud, then read it again by yourself.
“Grandpa, what is that in your pocket?” asked Nora.
“It is a cricket,” he said. “I found it under a stone in the garden.”
“Can I keep it as a pet? I love the chirping sound!”
“If you keep a cricket, you must take good care of it,” said Grandpa. “Crickets need to stay warm. They eat small pieces of fruit and vegetables. They also need a little dish of water.”

readings.title: Amal and the Little Garden Friends
readings.theme: Being curious about small creatures, caring for them, and letting go of fear.
readings.setting: A family garden behind Amal’s house, early on a warm morning.
readings.passageScript:
Early one warm morning, Amal went out to the garden behind her house. The sun was soft and gold. Birds sang from the tall tree by the wall. The air smelled of wet soil after the rain.
Amal loved the garden. But there was one thing she did not love: bugs. When a bee buzzed near her, she ran. When she saw a spider, she covered her eyes.
Her older brother Adam was already there. He sat very still on a low rock.
“Come, Amal,” he whispered. “Sit with me. If you look closely, the garden is full of little friends.”
Amal sat down slowly. On a bright red flower, a butterfly opened and closed its wings.
“Look,” said Adam. “A butterfly is an insect. It has six legs, and two antennae on its head. Its antennae help it feel and smell. It drinks sweet nectar from the flower.”
The butterfly did not seem scary at all. Amal smiled a little.
Then a bee flew above the flowers, going from one to the next.
“Is the bee going to sting me?” Amal asked, pulling back.
“No,” said Adam gently. “The bee is very busy. It is collecting nectar to make honey. Stay calm and do not wave your arms. Then the bee will leave you alone.”
Amal held still. The bee buzzed near her hand, then flew away. She let out a happy breath. She was not afraid now.
Near her foot, a long line of ants marched over the soil. They were carrying tiny seeds.
“Where are they going?” Amal asked.
“To their home,” said Adam. “Ants live in a big anthill under the ground. They work together and share everything. One ant is small, but many ants are very strong.”
Amal watched the ants for a long time. She liked how they helped each other, just like a big family.
Then she saw something between two branches of the small lemon tree. It was a web, shining with drops of water. In the middle sat a spider.
Amal wanted to run, but she did not.
“A spider is not an insect,” said Adam. “Count its legs.”
Amal counted, one by one. “Eight legs!” she said. “Insects have only six.”
“That is right,” said Adam, proud of her. “A spider spins its web from silk in its own body. The web catches insects for the spider to eat. It is not here to hurt you. It is only doing its work.”
Amal looked at the web. It was beautiful, like tiny threads of glass.
Just then, their little sister Mina came running. She lifted her foot to stamp on a fat worm on the path.
“Wait!” called Amal. “Do not step on it. A worm has no legs, but it is a good little animal. Worms crawl through the soil and help our plants grow strong.”
Mina stopped. Together, the three children watched the worm wriggle safely under a leaf.
That evening, Amal told her mother everything. “I used to be afraid of bugs,” she said. “But now I know they are little friends. The bee makes honey and the ants work together. The spider spins a web and the worm helps the soil.”
Her mother smiled. “When we understand something,” she said, “we stop being afraid of it. And when we care for even the smallest creature, our hearts grow bigger.”
Amal looked out at the dark garden. All her little friends were resting. She could not wait for morning, so she could watch them again.

comprehension.question: How many legs do all insects have, and what are their three main body parts called?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Six legs. The three main body parts are the head, the thorax and the abdomen.
comprehension.explanation: The second and third sentences of the text give both answers, one after the other.

comprehension.question: According to the text, what do antennae help insects do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They help insects feel and smell the things around them.
comprehension.explanation: The text says most insects have two antennae on their heads, and then explains what those antennae are for.

comprehension.question: The text says a spider and a worm are not insects. Give the reason for each one.
comprehension.correctAnswer: A spider has eight legs and a worm has no legs at all, but every insect has six legs.
comprehension.explanation: Two sentences near the end of the text each begin with the creature and then give a reason after the word because.

comprehension.question: In the poem, where is the bug at the start?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The bug is on the speaker, because the poem says, “There's a bug on me.”
comprehension.explanation: Line two of the poem tells you exactly where the bug is sitting.

comprehension.question: The poem asks, “Where did it go?” What answer does the very next line give?
comprehension.correctAnswer: “I don't know.” Nobody in the poem can say where the bug went.
comprehension.explanation: The poem is a short question and answer. Reading the last two lines together gives the reply.

comprehension.question: Find one pair of words in the poem that rhyme with each other.
comprehension.correctAnswer: “Three” and “me” rhyme, and “go” and “know” also rhyme.
comprehension.explanation: Poems often put rhyming words at the ends of lines. Say the last word of each line aloud to hear the pairs.

comprehension.question: What was in Grandpa's pocket, and where had he found it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A cricket, and he found it under a stone in the garden.
comprehension.explanation: Grandpa answers Nora's first question with both the creature and the place he found it.

comprehension.question: Why does Nora want to keep the cricket as a pet?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because she loves its chirping sound.
comprehension.explanation: Nora gives her reason in the same breath as her question about keeping the cricket.

comprehension.question: Grandpa names three things a pet cricket needs. What are they?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It needs to stay warm, it needs small pieces of fruit and vegetables, and it needs a little dish of water.
comprehension.explanation: Grandpa's last speech is a short list of three needs. A full answer names all three.

comprehension.question: At the start of the story, what did Amal do when a bee buzzed near her, and what did she do when she saw a spider?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She ran away from the bee, and she covered her eyes when she saw the spider.
comprehension.explanation: The second paragraph gives both reactions in two short sentences, one for each creature.

comprehension.question: What did Adam say a butterfly uses its antennae for?
comprehension.explanation: Adam explains the butterfly first, naming its six legs, its two antennae and what the antennae do.

comprehension.question: Adam told Amal how to stay safe near the bee. What did he tell her to do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Stay calm and do not wave your arms, and then the bee will leave you alone.
comprehension.explanation: Adam answers Amal's worried question about stinging with two clear pieces of advice.

comprehension.question: How did Amal know that the spider was not an insect?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She counted its legs one by one and found eight, and insects have only six.
comprehension.explanation: Adam asked her to count the legs, and her own words in the next line show what she found.

comprehension.question: What reason did Amal give Mina for not stepping on the worm?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A worm has no legs but it is a good little animal, because worms crawl through the soil and help plants grow strong.
comprehension.explanation: Amal calls out to Mina and then gives a reason, which shows how much she has learned.

comprehension.question: How did Amal change from the beginning of the story to the end? What helped her change?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She began afraid of bugs and ended calling them her little friends. Watching them closely with Adam, and having each creature explained, helped her change.
comprehension.explanation: Look for evidence from both ends of the story: running from the bee at the start, and her speech to her mother at the end.

comprehension.question: Amal's mother said, “When we understand something, we stop being afraid of it.” Tell about something you were afraid of before, but are not afraid of now.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept any personal example that names the thing, says what helped the child understand it, and says how they feel about it now.
comprehension.explanation: This question links the mother's words to the child's own life. Ask them to say what helped, just as Adam helped Amal.

comprehension.question: Amal watched the ants and said they were like a big family. What could you do at home or at school to care for small creatures?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept any kind, practical idea, such as stepping around a worm on the path, leaving a web alone, or moving a spider outside in a cup.
comprehension.explanation: Look for an idea the child could really do, and ask them to say which creature from the story it would help.

grammar.title: Position Words
grammar.explanation: Position words tell us where something is. When we watch bugs we need them to say exactly where each creature sits: on a leaf, under a rock, or inside a hole.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Choose the position word that matches where the bug really is.
on means touching the top: “The butterfly is on the flower.”
under means below: “The worm is under the rock.”
in means inside: “The ant is in the anthill.”
between means in the middle of two things: “The cricket is between two leaves.”
above means higher than, without touching: “The bee is above the flower.”
in front of means just before: “The spider is in front of its web.”
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to write “The ant is on the anthill.” when the ant has gone inside. Inside needs in, so we write “The ant is in the anthill.”
grammar.memoryTip: Use on when something touches the top of a thing, and in when something is inside it. A bug on a box sits on the lid; a bug in a box is shut inside.
grammar.practice:
Write the best position word (on, under, in, between, above, in front of) in each gap.
1. The butterfly is ______ the flower.
2. The worm is ______ the rock.
3. The ant is ______ the anthill.
4. The cricket is ______ two leaves.
5. The bee is flying ______ the flower.

grammar.title: Position Words: Map the Garden
grammar.explanation: Now you choose the position words yourself. A good garden report tells the reader exactly where each creature is, so that somebody who cannot see your picture still knows where to look.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Name the creature first, then the position word, then the thing it is near.
The ladybird is under the green leaf.
The snail is in front of the flowerpot.
Two bees are above the sunflower.
The web hangs between the gate and the wall.
grammar.commonMistake: Some writers stop at “The ant is there.” The word there does not tell the reader where to look, so name the place: “The ant is under the stone.”
grammar.memoryTip: Ask yourself three questions before you write: which creature, which position word, and near what? Answer all three and your sentence is complete.
grammar.practice: Draw a garden with six creatures in it. Write six sentences about your picture, using each position word once: on, under, in, between, above and in front of. Read your sentences to a family member and ask them to point to each creature.

grammar.title: Simple Present Tense for Facts
grammar.explanation: We use the simple present tense for facts, which are things that are always true. It is the tense we need for telling people what bugs have and what bugs do.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the plain verb with I, you, we, they and plural nouns.
Insects have six legs.
Bees make honey.
Spiders spin webs.
Add -s or -es to the verb with he, she, it and one single thing.
A butterfly has colourful wings.
A cricket makes a chirping sound.
A spider spins a web.
grammar.commonMistake: Writing “A spider spin a web.” leaves the verb bare. There is only one spider, so the verb takes an s: “A spider spins a web.”
grammar.memoryTip: One bug puts the s on the verb, and many bugs leave it off. Say it aloud: “One bee buzzes; two bees buzz.”
grammar.practice:
Write the correct form of the verb in brackets.
1. Butterflies ______ (fly) from flower to flower.
2. A spider ______ (spin) a web to catch insects.
3. Ants ______ (live) in colonies.
4. A cricket ______ (make) sounds with its wings.
5. Worms ______ (help) make the soil healthy.

grammar.title: Simple Present Tense: Write a Fact File
grammar.explanation: A fact file gives true information in short, clear sentences. Every sentence uses the simple present tense, so the reader knows the fact is always true and not just true today.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Count the creatures before you choose the verb.
One bee makes honey for the hive.
Many bees make honey for the hive.
One worm helps the soil stay healthy.
Many worms help the soil stay healthy.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often mix the two patterns, as in “Ants lives under the ground.” Ants is more than one, so the verb loses the s: “Ants live under the ground.”
grammar.memoryTip: Point at the naming word first. If you can put the word one in front of it, the verb needs an s.
grammar.practice: Choose three creatures from your word list. Write two true fact sentences about each one, so you have six sentences altogether. Write three sentences about a single creature and three about more than one, then check every verb ending.

grammar.title: Asking Questions with What, Where and How
grammar.explanation: Good learners ask questions. Each question word asks for a different kind of answer, so choosing the right one helps you find out exactly what you want to know.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Match the question word to the kind of answer you want.
What asks about a thing: “What do ants eat?” — “They eat leaves and seeds.”
Where asks about a place: “Where do spiders live?” — “They live in webs.”
How asks about a way of doing something: “How do bees help people?” — “They make honey.”
Every question you write ends with a question mark.
grammar.commonMistake: Asking “Where do bees help people?” asks for a place, but we wanted a way. Change the question word: “How do bees help people?”
grammar.memoryTip: What means a thing, Where means a place, and How means a way. Picture three doors and walk through the right one.
grammar.practice:
Write What, Where or How at the start of each question, then add a question mark.
1. ______ do crickets make their sound? — “By rubbing their wings.”
2. ______ do ants live? — “In an anthill under the ground.”
3. ______ do butterflies eat? — “Nectar from flowers.”

grammar.title: Asking Questions: Interview a Bug Expert
grammar.explanation: An interview is a set of questions asked one after another. When you plan your own questions you decide what you want to learn, and then you choose the question word that will bring that answer back.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Plan the answer you want, then pick the question word.
You want a thing: “What does a cricket eat?”
You want a place: “Where does a cricket sleep?”
You want a way: “How does a cricket make its chirping sound?”
Start every question with a capital letter and end it with a question mark.
grammar.commonMistake: A question written as a statement, such as “A cricket eats fruit.”, cannot be answered by anybody. Turn it around and add the question mark: “What does a cricket eat?”
grammar.memoryTip: Before you write, say out loud the answer you are hoping for. The kind of answer tells you which question word to use.
grammar.practice: Choose one creature from the unit. Write six questions to ask a bug expert about it: two beginning with What, two with Where and two with How. Ask a family member to answer three of them, then write their answers underneath.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Name and Describe a Bug
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one creature from your word list and get ready to talk about it.
Say “This is a ______.” or “This is an ______.” — choose an when the next word begins with a vowel sound, as in “an ant”.
Then say “It has ______ legs.”, “It lives ______.” and “It can ______.”
Record yourself and check three things: did you name the creature correctly, did you give the right number of legs, and did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, describe your creature to the tutor without naming it, and ask the tutor to guess which one it is.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Where Is the Bug?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look at a picture of a garden, or at your own garden drawing.
Point to one creature and say where it is, using a position word: “The butterfly is on the flower.”
Do the same for five more creatures, using a different position word each time.
Then ask a family member to close their eyes while you describe one creature, and see whether they can point to it.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to say where a bug is hiding, then repeat the sentence back and point to the same spot on your drawing. Keep your helper nearby.

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Bug Riddles
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play this guessing game with an adult or a friend.
Choose a creature, but keep its name secret.
Give three clues about it, such as “It has six legs. It can fly. It makes honey.”
Ask “What bug am I thinking of?”, let the other person guess, and then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Give the tutor your three riddle clues and ask it to guess your creature, then ask it to set a riddle for you while your helper listens.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Bug Scientist
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Be a scientist who explains how to tell an insect from other small animals.
Hold up a picture of a butterfly and say why it is an insect, naming the six legs, the three body parts and the two antennae.
Then hold up a spider and a worm and explain why neither one is an insect.
Record your explanation and listen back. Did you use the word because at least twice?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to name a small animal, then explain to it whether that animal is an insect and why. An adult should sit with you.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — My Garden Tour
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Take your family on a two-minute tour of a real garden or of your own drawing.
Introduce each creature you meet: “Here is a bee. It collects nectar and it makes honey.”
Use a position word for every creature so your listener knows where to look.
Finish by saying which creature you like best and why, then record your tour.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Read your garden tour to the tutor with an adult present, and ask it which sentence gave the clearest picture and why.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — Bug Question Circle
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Sit in a circle with your family or your class.
Ask the person on your left one question beginning with What, Where or How, such as “How does a spider catch its food?”
Listen to the answer, say thank you, and then answer the question you are asked.
Keep the circle going until everybody has asked and answered twice, and record the last round.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor three questions about bugs, one starting with What, one with Where and one with How, then ask it which question word gave the most useful answer.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Describe a Bug
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose one creature and write three or four sentences about it. Answer these questions inside your sentences: What is it called? How many legs does it have? What colour is it? Where does it live, and what can it do?
writing.modelText: A butterfly is a colourful insect. It has six legs and two wings. Butterflies fly from flower to flower. They drink sweet nectar.
writing.successCriteria: My sentences name the creature, give the number of legs, say where it lives and say what it can do, and each one starts with a capital letter and ends with a full stop.
writing.support: Read the meaning of your creature on your word list first, then turn it into your own sentence.
writing.extension: Add one more sentence that begins with because to say why you chose this creature.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Where Do Bugs Live?
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six sentences and use a different position word in each one. These frames will help you: The ______ is on the ______. The ______ is under the ______. The ______ is in the ______. The ______ is between the ______. The ______ is above the ______. The ______ is in front of the ______.
writing.modelText: The bee is on the sunflower. The worm is under the flowerpot. The ant is in the anthill. The web is between the two branches. The butterfly is above the wall. The snail is in front of the gate.
writing.successCriteria: I used all six position words, each one only once, and every sentence names a creature and a place.
writing.support: Draw the garden picture first, then write down what you can see in it.
writing.extension: Add a describing word to three of your sentences, such as “the tall sunflower”.

writing.title: Story response: Amal and the Little Garden Friends
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write about the story in three parts. First write two sentences saying how Amal felt about bugs at the start and at the end. Then write two sentences explaining why the spider and the worm are not insects. Finally draw the moment Amal stopped Mina, and write one caption under your picture.
writing.modelText:
At the start Amal was afraid of bugs and she ran from the bee. At the end she called them her little friends.
A spider is not an insect, because it has eight legs. A worm is not an insect, because it has no legs at all.
Amal asks Mina to step over the worm on the path.
writing.successCriteria: My first two sentences show how Amal changed, my next two sentences use because, and my caption names both Amal and Mina.
writing.support: Reread the first two paragraphs and the last three paragraphs of the story before you write.
writing.extension: Write one more sentence saying what Amal might tell her friend Nora about the garden the next day.

writing.title: Writing 4 — My Insect Fact Card
writing.promptAndInstructions: Make a fact card for one insect. Draw the insect large in the middle, label the head, the thorax, the abdomen, the six legs and the two antennae, then write three fact sentences underneath in the simple present tense.
writing.modelText:
Labels: head, thorax, abdomen, legs, antennae.
A bee has six legs and three body parts. A bee collects nectar from flowers. Bees make honey inside the hive.
writing.successCriteria: My card has all five labels spelled correctly, and my three facts use the simple present tense with the right verb ending.
writing.support: Use the reading “What Is an Insect?” to check the names of the three body parts.
writing.extension: Add a heading and one question that your reader could answer from your card.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Insect or Not?
writing.promptAndInstructions: Sort six creatures into two lists headed Insects and Not insects: butterfly, spider, bee, worm, cricket, ant. Then write one sentence about each creature in the Not insects list, using because to give your reason.
writing.modelText:
Insects: butterfly, bee, cricket, ant.
Not insects: spider, worm.
A spider is not an insect, because it has eight legs and not six.
A worm is not an insect, because it has no legs and no wings.
writing.sentenceStarter: A spider is not an insect, because
writing.successCriteria: Both lists are correct, and each of my two sentences uses because and gives a reason about legs.
writing.support: Count the legs on each creature in your picture book before you sort them.
writing.extension: Add a snail to your lists and write one sentence explaining where it belongs.

writing.title: Writing 6 — A Tiny Garden Story
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write your own short garden story in six sentences. Begin with a child who does not like one creature, show the child watching it closely, and finish with the child changing their mind. Use at least three unit words and two position words.
writing.modelText: Leo did not like spiders. One morning he sat on the step and watched. A spider was spinning a web between two branches. Leo counted eight thin legs. The web shone above the wet grass. Now Leo says the spider is his garden friend.
writing.expectedLength: 6 sentences with a beginning, middle and end
writing.successCriteria: My story has a beginning, a middle and an end, uses three unit words and two position words, and every sentence is punctuated.
writing.support: Plan your three parts out loud before you write, using the story “Amal and the Little Garden Friends” as your model.
writing.extension: Give your story a title and read it aloud to somebody at home.

activities.title: Activity 1 — Fill in the Blanks (Bug Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write out each sentence and fill the gap with the missing word.
1. All insects have ______ legs.
2. Spiders have ______ legs, so they are not insects.
3. Insects have ______ main body parts.
4. Bees collect ______ from flowers to make honey.
5. Ants live together in groups called ______.

activities.title: Activity 2 — True or False (Insect Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sentence. If it is false, write the correct sentence underneath.
1. All insects have eight legs.
2. Butterflies are insects.
3. Spiders are insects.
4. Worms are not insects.
activities.answerSummary: 1. False, because insects have six legs. 2. True. 3. False, because spiders have eight legs. 4. True.

activities.title: Activity 3 — Fill in the Position Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Complete each sentence with the best position word from this list: on, under, in, between, above, in front of.
1. The butterfly is ______ the flower.
2. The worm is ______ the rock.
3. The ant is ______ the anthill.
4. The cricket is ______ two leaves.

activities.title: Activity 4 — Build an Insect
activities.instructionsAndItems: Build a model insect from paper, clay or craft sticks. It must have three body parts joined in a line, six legs on the middle part and two antennae on the head. Write a label for each part, place the labels beside your model, then show it to somebody and name every part aloud.
activities.answerSummary: A correct model has three body parts, six legs joined to the middle part and two antennae on the head, with all five labels spelled as they are on the word list.

activities.title: Activity 5 — Habitat Match
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each creature to the place where it lives. Draw a line between the pairs, or write them out.
Creatures: ant, spider, worm, bee, cricket, butterfly
Places: an anthill, a web, the soil, a hive, long grass, a flower garden
activities.answerSummary: ant → an anthill, spider → a web, worm → the soil, bee → a hive, cricket → long grass, butterfly → a flower garden

activities.title: Activity 6 — Position-Word Trail
activities.instructionsAndItems: Hide a small toy bug somewhere at home. Write six clues that lead a family member to it, using a different position word in every clue, such as “Look under the cushion.” and “The next clue is in the shoe box.” Give the clues out one at a time and watch your reader follow the trail.
activities.answerSummary: Six clues that each use a different position word from on, under, in, between, above and in front of, and that lead in order to the hidden toy.

assignments.title: All About Bugs learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together three pieces of work. First complete “Writing 4 — My Insect Fact Card” with all five labels and three fact sentences. Next record “Speaking 4 — Bug Scientist”, explaining why a butterfly is an insect and why a spider is not. Finally complete “Writing 2 — Where Do Bugs Live?” using each of the six position words once. Check every piece against the writing and speaking rubrics before you submit them together.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: Which of these is a small animal with eight legs that spins a web?
quizzes.explanation: A spider has eight legs and spins a web. A cricket is a six-legged insect, nectar is a sweet drink and an anthill is a home.

quizzes.question: Which insect collects nectar from flowers and makes honey?
quizzes.explanation: A bee collects nectar and makes honey. A web is what a spider spins, above is a position word and crawl is an action word.

quizzes.question: How many legs does every insect have?
quizzes.explanation: All insects have six legs. Two legs belong to a bird, eight legs belong to a spider, and a worm is the creature with no legs.

quizzes.question: Why is a spider not an insect?
quizzes.options: It has eight legs, and insects have six. | It has wings, and insects have no wings. | It lives in the soil, and insects live in trees. | It makes honey, and insects do not make honey.
quizzes.correctAnswer: It has eight legs, and insects have six.
quizzes.explanation: Counting the legs is the test. Insects have six legs, so a spider with eight legs belongs to a different family of animals.

quizzes.question: A butterfly is sitting and touching the top of a flower. Which position word is correct?
quizzes.explanation: On means touching the top of something. Under means below it, between needs two things, and above means higher up without touching.

quizzes.question: Which sentence tells you that the ant has gone inside its home?
quizzes.options: The ant is on the anthill. | The ant is above the anthill. | The ant is in the anthill. | The ant is in front of the anthill.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The ant is in the anthill.
quizzes.explanation: In means inside. On means touching the top, above means higher up, and in front of means just before it.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the simple present tense correctly?
quizzes.options: A spider spin a web. | A spider spins a web. | A spider spinning a web. | A spiders spin a web.
quizzes.explanation: There is only one spider, so the verb takes an s. The other sentences leave the s off, have no helping word, or add the s to the naming word instead.

quizzes.question: Choose the correct verb for this fact: “Bees ______ honey.”
quizzes.explanation: Bees is more than one, so the verb stays plain. Makes belongs with one bee, made is the past, and making needs a helping word.

quizzes.question: Which question asks about the place where spiders live?
quizzes.options: What do spiders eat? | Where do spiders live? | How do spiders spin a web? | Why do spiders hide?
quizzes.explanation: Where always asks about a place. What asks about a thing, How asks about a way of doing something, and Why asks for a reason.

quizzes.question: What does the word antennae mean?
quizzes.options: The net of thin silk that a spider spins to catch insects. | The home that ants build under the ground. | The body parts that some insects use for flying. | The two thin parts on an insect's head that help it feel and smell.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The two thin parts on an insect's head that help it feel and smell.
quizzes.explanation: Antennae sit on the head and help an insect feel and smell. The other three options describe a web, an anthill and wings.

liveSessions.title: All About Bugs: bug names and insect facts
liveSessions.beforeSession: Watch the teacher lecture and learn Group 1 of the word list: butterfly, cricket, ant, bee, spider and worm.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome and bug-sound warm-up; 10 min naming the six creatures from pictures; 10 min reading “What Is an Insect?” together; 15 min Speaking 1 in pairs; 5 min reflection
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 1 — Name and Describe a Bug and upload it.

liveSessions.title: Shared reading: Amal and the Little Garden Friends
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the story once at home and underline every unit word that you meet.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min story recap; 15 min shared reading aloud with attention to speech marks; 15 min story questions 10 to 14; 10 min hot-seating Amal and Adam
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer the three Think and talk questions in your book.

liveSessions.title: Grammar workshop: position words
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read Grammar 1 and try the five position-word gaps on your own.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min hide-the-bug warm-up; 10 min the six position words with real objects; 15 min drawing and describing a garden map; 10 min writing six sentences; 5 min sharing
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Grammar 4 and finish your garden map.

liveSessions.title: Grammar and speaking: true facts about bugs
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read Grammar 2 and learn the one bug, many bugs verb rule.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min “One bee buzzes; two bees buzz” chant; 15 min sorting single and plural fact cards; 15 min modelling the bug scientist explanation; 10 min paired explaining

liveSessions.title: Guided writing: insect fact cards
liveSessions.beforeSession: Reread “What Is an Insect?” and choose the insect for your fact card.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min labelled-diagram review; 10 min modelling three fact sentences; 20 min guided writing of the fact card; 10 min partner proofreading
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Writing 4 — My Insect Fact Card and check every verb ending.

liveSessions.title: All About Bugs: question circle and review
liveSessions.beforeSession: Write six questions for the question circle, two each beginning with What, Where and How.

liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.

teacherNotes.noteType: AI tutor use and device safety
teacherNotes.note: Every AI tutor task in this unit assumes an adult is present. Never leave a child alone with the device: sit beside them, keep the screen visible, and close the session together. The prompts below are drawn from the source pack, grouped by file. Vocabulary: “What is the difference between a bug and an insect?”, “How can I tell if a small animal is an insect?”, “Why is a spider not an insect?”, “When do I use on and when do I use in?”, “What other verbs can I use to talk about what bugs do?”, “What other bugs might I find in my own garden?” Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?”, “When do I add -s to a verb for facts?”, “Can you give me five more sentences to practise position words?” Reading and story: “Can you read the text about insects with me and explain the hard words?”, “What does chirping mean?”, “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?” Writing: “Can you check if my bug description is correct?”, “What other details can I add to make my writing more interesting?”

Some children are genuinely frightened of insects, so never ask a child to hold or touch a live creature. Watching from a safe distance, using photographs or using a paper model is always enough, and every creature should be left exactly where it was found. Children who cannot go outside can complete every task from the pictures in the unit. Check for allergies and stings before any outdoor watching, and agree a look, do not touch rule with the whole group first. When marking, treat a missing verb ending as the next step rather than as a mistake, and praise any child who gives a reason with because even if the spelling wobbles. The story carries the unit's value: when we understand a creature, we stop being afraid of it.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. six, 2. eight, 3. three, 4. nectar, 5. colonies. The whole sentence must be copied out, not just the missing word.
1. False, insects have six legs. 2. True. 3. False, spiders have eight legs and so are not insects. 4. True, worms have no legs at all.
1. on, 2. under, 3. in, 4. between. Accept above for item 1 only if the child has drawn the butterfly hovering clear of the flower.
1. on, 2. under, 3. in, 4. between, 5. above.
1. fly, 2. spins, 3. live, 4. makes, 5. help. Items 2 and 4 are single creatures, so those two verbs take an s.
1. How, 2. Where, 3. What. Each question must also end with a question mark.
Six legs, and the three main body parts are the head, the thorax and the abdomen. Award the mark when both halves are given.
They help insects feel and smell the things around them. Accept feel and smell on their own.
A spider has eight legs and a worm has no legs, while every insect has six. Both reasons are needed for the mark.
On the speaker, quoting line two of the poem: “There's a bug on me.”
“I don't know.” Accept the idea that nobody in the poem can say where the bug went.
Either pair earns the mark: three and me, or go and know.
A cricket, found under a stone in the garden. Both the creature and the place are needed.
Because she loves the chirping sound that a cricket makes.
Warmth, small pieces of fruit and vegetables, and a little dish of water. Award the mark for all three.
She ran away from the bee, and she covered her eyes when she saw the spider.
To feel and to smell. Accept feeling and smelling things around it.
Stay calm and do not wave your arms, and then the bee will leave you alone. Both parts of the advice are needed.
She counted eight legs, and insects have only six. Accept counting the legs as the method.
A worm has no legs but it is still a good animal, because worms crawl through the soil and help plants grow strong.

From afraid to fond: she ran from the bee at the start and called the creatures her little friends at the end. The mark needs the change plus one cause, such as sitting still with Adam or having each creature explained.
Personal answer. Award the mark when the child names the thing, says what helped them understand it, and says how they feel about it now.
Any kind, practical idea, for example stepping around a worm, leaving a web alone, or carrying a spider outside in a cup. Ask which creature from the story it would help.
spider. Eight legs and a silk web belong to the spider, not to the six-legged cricket.
bee. Only the bee turns nectar into honey; the other three options are a thing, a position word and an action word.
six. Six legs is the test that separates insects from spiders and worms.
It has eight legs, and insects have six. Reject any answer about wings or honey.
on. Touching the top of a thing is exactly what on means.

Three or four sentences that name the creature, give the number of legs, name a colour, say where it lives and say what it can do. This piece is also the warm-up for the unit portfolio, so check the punctuation before the child moves on to Writing 4.
Six sentences about the child's own drawing, one for each of on, under, in, between, above and in front of. Reject any sentence that uses the word there in place of a real position.
Six fact sentences about three creatures: three about a single creature, with the s on the verb, and three about more than one, with the plain verb.
Six questions about one creature: two beginning with What, two with Where and two with How, each with a capital letter and a question mark, plus three written answers.
Listen for six legs, three body parts and two antennae named for the butterfly, then a reason for the spider (eight legs) and for the worm (no legs). The word because should appear at least twice.
The tour must introduce each creature, use a position word every time so the listener knows where to look, and end with a favourite creature and a reason.
Each child asks and answers twice. Mark the match between the question word and the answer given: What brings a thing, Where brings a place and How brings a way.
Five labels (head, thorax, abdomen, legs, antennae) spelled as on the word list, plus three simple present facts with correct verb endings.
Insects: butterfly, bee, cricket, ant. Not insects: spider, worm. Then two because sentences, one naming eight legs and one naming no legs.
A six-sentence story with a clear beginning, middle and end, at least three unit words and two position words, and a child who changes their mind by the last sentence.
Three body parts in a line, six legs on the middle part and two antennae on the head, with five correctly spelled labels placed beside the model.
ant → an anthill, spider → a web, worm → the soil, bee → a hive, cricket → long grass, butterfly → a flower garden.
Six clues in order, each using a different position word, and each one true of the place where the toy is really hidden.
The ant is in the anthill. In is the only position word that means inside.
A spider spins a web. One spider takes the s on the verb.
make. Bees is plural, so the verb stays plain.
Where do spiders live? Where is the question word that asks for a place.
The two thin parts on an insect's head that help it feel and smell.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name common bugs and garden animals.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what makes an animal an insect.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain why a spider and a worm are not insects.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use position words to say where a bug is.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the simple present tense for facts.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can write a simple description of a bug.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

rubrics.level3: Mostly clear and easy to follow

rubrics.level2: Some creature names used, usually prompted
rubrics.level3: Most creature, body-part and position words used correctly
rubrics.level4: Wide range of unit words used naturally

rubrics.level2: Verb endings correct after a reminder
rubrics.level4: Simple present verbs always correct, singular and plural

rubrics.level1: Needs full support to take a turn
rubrics.level3: Asks and answers questions in turn
rubrics.level4: Asks, answers and adds a new fact of their own

rubrics.level1: Ideas unclear or away from the task
rubrics.level2: One fact about the creature, with little detail
rubrics.level4: Clear facts with well chosen detail

rubrics.level2: Some order, with gaps in the sequence
rubrics.level4: Strong order using labels, lists or a beginning, middle and end

rubrics.level3: Good use of creature, body-part and position words
rubrics.level4: Precise word choices that suit the fact card or story

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and full stops mostly missing
rubrics.level2: Some capital letters and full stops
rubrics.level3: Capital letters, spaces and full stops usually correct
rubrics.level4: Accurate punctuation, question marks and verb endings
